author: @iancrawford3606
text:
Getting to the driver of your question - power harvesting - wind or solar ?
The storage in battery.
I hate lithium. Hard rock lithium production is the dirtiest game in town.
As you know I love zinc technologies. There’s hundreds of times more zinc on the planet than lithium.
Zinc air is the cheapest safest energy storage going.
Wonderful in hearing aids.
No nasty halogens.
BUT there is still that problem of dendrite formation on the electrode which reduces efficiency. That’s why the commercial boyos have gone for pumped halogens.
But .., nano … chemistries … and we are working on geometries will solve this problem - and then EVs will be as cheap as minis were ~ oh those were the days.
I’m hooked on ye olde hexagon as holding the secret …
And…. Just learnt that those masters of hexagonal construction - bees - first have a mob of workers that form the wax in circles first - so combo of circle / hexagon probably holds the secret to zinc air batteries .
Off you go 😎

author: @iancrawford3606
text:
Thanks mate you are absolutely spot on. But then you mostly are !! It’s really probability math at work in your subconscious without realising !!
Ha ha I made a decision to try to solubilise something which was very difficult. Wrong decision. But I learnt a lot about Fenton solution. And found a guy in Poland that had access to all Fentons actual notes. Which explained why sometimes it worked perfectly but 
other times not. All due to getting the iron sulfate in solution which requires water to be boiled to ensure the 
H2O is just that !! Meaning that 90% of all water treatment plants around the world aren’t using the best Fenton making the hydrogen peroxide guys squillions ha ha - 
And then…. Good old Archimedes entered. And the higher the solids I used in the solution of fine particles they didn’t  drop out. No need to solubilise them in the first place. Chuck in some xanthan. 
Gravity and density and that bath !! 
Cheers

author: @shortbuslife3440
text:
So far what I have found is the solar panels that I have have worked flawlessly for the last 4 years with no issues and no maintenance required, however the wind turbines I have bought haven't produced any noticeable power, as in not even managed to produce even 1 watt due to insufficient voltage in most cases admittedly these are small VAWT's of around 500w (not sure where they get that figure from) but in wind speeds (on the more expensive version made in Italy) upto 25mph on a farm in Scotland and on a Chinese Lantern 600w version during a hurricane last year upto 90mph ish, neither were able to reach required voltage or produce a significant current and I have been very disappointed with the results.

Upon testing of my own design (based on the cross flow fan design) on there motors/generators I found my turbine was able to start up earlier and reach peak voltage much sooner but it appears the motors/generators being used are designed for too low a voltage at too high a rpm.

So in conclusion with extensive testing and running and installing off grid renewable technologies plus living off grid, at the moment I would say solar is far more reliable and efficient but once I have a working VAWT maybe I'll change my mind and maybe you could try mine out Robert?

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
Figuring out if your location had good winds can be hard, that 25mph doesn't help if it is not steady and the 90mph storm causes safety brakes to activate. But there seem to be plenty of turbines on sale that are either very badly designed or outright scams and if you got another turbine to work there then it seems to be a hardware issue.

Most turbines assume 5-10m/s winds to get the rated output, but apparently laws aren't clear on that so technically I cold sell you a table fan as a 3000W wind turbine on basis that technically you might get some power out of it in a hurricane, and 1W is included in "produces up to 3000W".

Compare that to some scammer trying to sell you any solar panel that claims to get more than 1000W/m2. Everyone knows that is the maximum incoming energy from sunlight and it is easy enough to set up lamps to properly test how much energy a panel really produces. PV is definitely far easier for common person to use.

replies.author: @shortbuslife3440
replies.text:
@AnalystPrime At 135m elevation on top of a hill surrounded by fields with a few trees  plus a barn there is a little turbulance and the 25mph wind was the constant wind gusting quite a bit higher, as for the breaking effect this would only start on HAWT once wind was high enough but VAWT's are self breaking so can't exceed more than about 130rpm about a 3rd of the speed of a HAWT.
Bearing in mind I'm designing my own VAWT and am researching this subject very heavily so I can produce something that works plus I sell and install both wind and solar at the moment I'd still say solar is superior and as you say 'far easier for common person to use'.
I'm hoping to get a working turbine ready to sell in the next few months but it takes time with the research into aerofoil design, manufacturing and of coarse the generator as well.

I'll add a big thankyou to Robert for all the ideas he has given me.

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
@shortbuslife3440 Sounds like a pretty ideal location for wind. If those turbines were properly installed and claim to work at those wind speeds then there is clearly something wrong, have you checked online if other customers have similar experiences and if the company has fixed the issues?

Wind is good choice when it works. Problems include higher price per watt and you can't exactly fine tune things later. If part of your panels are shaded by a tree that you can't or won't cut down you can just move some panels and they will work fine; if you built your tower twenty feet too low and on the wrong side of your house, tough luck, you can either take what you got or pay full cost for another tower. Also, NIMBYS making trouble about panels on your roof ruining their nice view is a very different issue from someone wanting to put up a tall tower in the middle of a suburb.

replies.author: @shortbuslife3440
replies.text:
@AnalystPrime Yes it turns out the chinese lantern type are known to be bad in one video a company in America attached one to the roof of a car to test it and at 60mph it produced very little power I think it was about 60w at 12v and the turbine was supposed to be 600w and he had tested it at various different speeds up till then but wasn't prepared to go any faster, I don't blame him.
As for the Domus there are no reviews of it, but I'll be running more tests and may do a review, I can add that the build quality of the Chinese one was far superior as this one doesn't even come with stainless bolts and a tiny m5 bolt to hold rotor onto generator that came off in a very short time so needed lock tight. It was also poorly balanced causing alot of vibration at speed.

author: @Marian_Martin_Morgenstern
text:
scio me nihil scire
But what the World and Dialectics taught me is, that there are no absolutes and nothing remains true forever. But People are People and many only accept the absolute, that all they know is all there is to know and believe that what they believe to be true is true for everyone, everywhere and evermore.
And if you really want to know which is better, Wind or Solar, I can only say:

 "Yes"

And did you know that there are plans for a Solar Wind Power Station? Its basically a big greenhouse with a high chimney in the middle of it. And if you place a wind turbine into the chimney you should get a very efficient Power generation going, driven by the hot air, heated by the sun in the greenhouse. Works practically all day and into a good part of the night, as long as the earth in the "greenhouse" part is still warmer then the surrounding area. And the bigger the "greenhouse", and the higher the chimney, the more power can be produced.
So is that Solar or is that Wind?
Also you have a greenhouse in which you can plant food as added bonus

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Assuming availability of items and/or materials/resources, after the particular end effect you want (heat/electricity etc); generally two main things influence what you/I do, 1) is 'it' needed NOW, 2) can 'it' be made by you.
If 1, then you have to either trust an 'expert' and get your wallet out, or you have to have worked in the trade (could still need wallet).
If 2, then (unless you are in the trade, which is effectively experienced/experimented) it's useful to research others experience/experiments, and/or do several small scale experiments and choose which one(s) to scale up.  You can always tinker and change and expand things later as long as 1, 2 or both achieve the original objective(s).
Without 1, 2 usually the only thing you'll get in a rush are accidents and kids.  ;-)

author: @ArtisticImpressionsbyBobRouth
text:
It's a circular argument.  It's all perspective and purpose, cause and effect, wish vs want.  1st what's the purpose of the project for the individual. I think we have a group of diversified desires. Are you trying to answer a tech question or asking because you need to fill a personal need. If you are lucky enough to live where it is always sunny and windy and your property borders a moving year round stream... there is no reason you should be paying anyone for your electrical needs.
I always assume I will do all of the above, if possible. solar, wind or water.  The goal is to get something from nothing for virtually nothing.  Needs vs results.

author: @Equelan2
text: Both energy harvesting methods has their own advantages and disadvantages. So it depends of the need and expectation of the people who have to choose one way or other to invest. I think a hybrid system supported with some lead acid and zinc br batteries is the best option to have a dependable of grid home electricity. Because wind may not blow everyday also Sun may be blocked by chemtrails even earth batteries will oxide your electrodes in time they have to need constant cleaning and replenising electrolyte. Beside those three methods, investing into solid state batteries may give amazing results and benefits depends on the materials and electrolyte used to obtain power. For example imagine you have found a way to make solid battery bricks. They are good as building elements also they are producing amazing amperes and voltages with life long durability. Constructing walls of house with that type of solid state bricks may provide lifelong cheap and unlimited energy to those houses. It can also be patentable and be sold as a product to other countries.

author: @MrBrelindm
text:
Very wise words indeed! What ever happened to conservation of energy (the concept). Seems to have fallen by the wayside in favor of how much energy can each human possibly use on the daily.

Couldn't we just commit to not being energy whores instead?

It would be handy to see your individual daily average energy consumption. But to do this you need equivalent units of measure for each source. Converting every energy source to its electrical equivalent makes this possible. Include gasoline (petrol for you Brits) for transport, natural gas for heating and cooking, and add that to your easily obtainable electrical usage to get your personal total daily average energy usage. You will be absolutely shocked at how energy intensive your existence actually is - I guarantee it! I did this for myself and discovered that on average, I alone consume over 124KW per day!

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
Whilst I would not disagree with the spirit that Rob expounds, in industry this ‘well it seemed like a good idea at the time” approach is an anathema to good project planning. For those suck in  Buridan's ass mode I suggest they write out the all the pros & cons that the can think of like cost, space available, etc. Do it as a ‘Decision Tree’. Wikipedia (as always) has an article by that name to provide the general idea.

[Buridan's ass is an illustration of a parado in philosophy in the conception of free will. It refers to a hypothetical situation wherein an ass that is equally hungry and thirsty is placed precisely midway between a stack of hay and a pail of water. Since the paradox assumes the ass will always go to whichever is closer, it dies of both hunger and thirst since it cannot make any rational decision between the hay and water.]

I found that if this approach still failed to yield a clear ‘best option’ then I’d resort to brute force statistics which is 50% reliable. Method: (1) Borrow a coin. (2) Toss it. (3) Immediately start work based on outcome. (4) Giving coin back — based on second toss.

author: @gordonlawrence1448
text: Surely it depends on where you site the source? EG where I live I'm in a low wind area. So that makes solar better. In the Islands off Scotland then wind is more steady so I would think wind turbines are better. If you are in a windy area but space is limited then vertical is better than horizontal. Also off some coasts there is wave/tidal potential. In Iceland you also have geothermal.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sure - find your reason and go for it

replies.author: @YathishShamaraj
replies.text: In the southern peninsula of India we have 6 months of dry open hot skies and 6 months of monsoon winds and rain clouds...we need either one for each season

author: @valleywrestler20
text: I think it all depends on where you live, if your in a hot arid area, solar is best during day, then wind is best at night because of dropping of temperature. if your in area thats mostly cloudy, rain not very sunny, wind is best during day and night, and solar will not be very efficient. Solar is enticing because it is a sit and forget type of energy, barely any maintenance or hassle, no mechanical moving parts, but its only effective if the sun is out on a clear day. wind however can be a problem with maintenance since you need to grease them, replace parts and blades, plus theres alot of controls on them to make sure they dont break themselves in high winds. But even small scale wind does produce alot of power but as long as there is wind, dont live in a windy area, its not very effective. I like to go with solar because you barely have to do anything with them, solar panels are cheap but the problem is the setup is not. Inverters and batteries are very expensive the bigger you go. All in all you have to look at your environment, and whats available to you, especially also when it comes to laws and policies. Look at your situation is more than anything else when it comes to renewable energy.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it depends on what reason you find - if where you live works for you awesome
